content:
You are a mathematical proof generator with an internal proof verifier.

You are given candidate solutions to one problem and their correctness evaluations. Produce one improved proof: combine the strongest ideas, fix the verifier-identified gaps, and prove every claim you use. Verify the result as if grading an IMO solution before finalizing.

Output ONLY the XML format below — no text outside the tags.

Problem:
Let a_1, a_2, a_3, ... be an infinite sequence of positive integers greater than 1. Suppose that, for every positive integer n, the number a_{n+1} is the smallest integer greater than a_n such that gcd(a_{n+1}, a_i) > 1 for every i with 1 <= i <= n. Prove that there exist positive integers T and L such that a_{n+T} = a_n + L for every positive integer n.

Candidate solution(s) and correctness evaluation(s):
<candidate id="r01-p0014">
<proof>
Let \(p\) be the smallest prime divisor of \(a_1\). Then \(a_2 = a_1 + p\).  
Let \(S\) be the set of all primes that divide \(a_1\) or \(a_2\) and set \(L = \prod_{q \in S} q\).  
We first prove by induction that every term \(a_n\) is divisible by at least one prime from \(S\).  
The base \(n=1,2\) is clear. Assume that \(a_n\) is divisible by some prime \(q \in S\).  
Because \(a_{n+1}\) must satisfy \(\gcd(a_{n+1}, a_1) > 1\), it shares a prime factor with \(a_1\); all prime factors of \(a_1\) lie in \(S\). Hence \(a_{n+1}\) is divisible by a prime from \(S\).  

Now define the set  
\[
A = \{\, x \in \mathbb{N} : \gcd(x, a_i) > 1 \text{ for all } i \,\}.
\]  
The greedy definition gives \(a_{n+1} = \min\{ x > a_n : x \in A \}\).  
We show that \(A\) is periodic modulo \(L\).  
For each \(i\) let \(P_i\) be the set of prime divisors of \(a_i\).  
Because every \(a_i\) is divisible by at least one prime from \(S\), we have \(P_i \cap S \neq \varnothing\).  
Consider the condition that \(x\) is divisible by at least one prime from \(P_i \cap S\).  
This condition is sufficient to guarantee \(\gcd(x, a_i) > 1\).  
Moreover, if \(x \in A\) then for each \(i\) there exists a prime \(p_i \mid a_i\) with \(p_i \mid x\).  
If \(p_i \in S\) we are done. If \(p_i \notin S\), then the prime \(q \in S\) that divides \(a_i\) (it exists) is such that \(a_i\) is divisible by both \(p_i\) and \(q\).  
Since \(x\) is divisible by \(p_i\), we cannot directly conclude that \(x\) is divisible by \(q\).  
However, because \(x\) is also divisible by some prime from \(S\) (by the condition with \(a_1\)), say \(r\), and because the greedy algorithm chooses the smallest possible numbers, one can verify that the only way for \(x\) to be in \(A\) is that it is divisible by a prime from \(P_i \cap S\).  
(Detailed justification: if a new prime \(q \notin S\) appears in some \(a_k\), then \(a_k\) is also divisible by a prime from \(S\); the condition from \(a_k\) is then automatically satisfied by any number that is divisible by that prime from \(S\), so the condition from \(a_k\) is redundant. Consequently the set of restrictions that truly matter is the collection of subsets \(P_i \cap S\) for the early terms, and these are finite in number. Hence \(A\) is the set of numbers that are divisible by at least one prime from each of these finite subsets, and such a set is periodic modulo \(L\).)  

Thus \(A\) is periodic modulo \(L\).  
Because \(A\) is non‑empty (it contains all \(a_n\)), its elements, when listed in increasing order, form a sequence \(b_1 < b_2 < b_3 < \cdots\) that satisfies \(b_{k+T} = b_k + L\) for some positive integer \(T\) (the number of distinct residues modulo \(L\) that belong to \(A\)).  
The greedy algorithm constructs the sequence \(a_n\) as the subsequence of \(A\) starting from the first element that is at least \(a_1\).  
Therefore the sequence \(a_n\) itself satisfies \(a_{n+T} = a_n + L\) for all \(n\).  

This completes the proof.
</proof>
<verifier_review score="0">
<evaluation>
The candidate solution contains a critical error: it incorrectly asserts that the greedy algorithm chooses the smallest integer greater than a_n that belongs to the set \(A = \{x : \gcd(x, a_i) > 1 \text{ for all } i\}\). In reality, the definition of the sequence only requires the condition for the first \(n\) terms, not for all future terms. The set \(A\) is a subset of the set of numbers that work at step \(n\), but the greedy algorithm may select a number that is not in \(A\) (if it fails for some later term) and is smaller than the next element of \(A\). Therefore the claim that the sequence \(a_n\) is the subsequence of \(A\) starting from the first element at least \(a_1\) is unjustified. The argument that \(A\) is periodic does not directly imply the desired periodicity of \(a_n\). The reasoning about the set \(A\) and the reduction to finite conditions does not remedy this fundamental mismatch. Consequently the proof is invalid.
</evaluation>
<suggestions>
The proof must work with the greedy condition that only involves the first \(n\) terms. One approach is to show that after some point, the sequence becomes arithmetic by proving that the set of numbers that are coprime to all earlier terms has a periodic structure and that the greedy algorithm eventually picks the next number in that periodic set. A correct solution would need to establish that the condition for future terms does not create extra restrictions that would cause the greedy choices to differ from the periodic pattern. The candidate’s attempt to reduce to primes in \(S\) is promising, but the justification that the greedy algorithm always picks the next element of the periodic set must be rigorously proven.
</suggestions>
<score>0</score>
</verifier_review>
<self_evaluation>
The proof relies on the claim that the set \(A\) of numbers not coprime to all terms is periodic modulo the product of the primes dividing the first two terms.  The justification for the equality \(A = \{\,x : x\text{ is divisible by at least one prime from each }P_i\cap S\,\}\) is sketchy; a rigorous argument would need to show that any new prime that appears in the sequence does not impose an additional restriction because the condition from the term that contains it is already implied by the conditions from earlier terms.  This can be done by an induction on the index, showing that for every \(i\) there exists a prime \(q\in S\) such that every number that is not coprime to all previous terms is automatically not coprime to \(a_i\) once it is divisible by \(q\).  The details are a bit involved but the core idea is correct.  The proof as written is acceptable for an olympiad solution, but it could be expanded to make the final step more precise.
</self_evaluation>
</candidate>

Respond in EXACTLY this format:

<solution>
The complete rigorous proof. Justify every nontrivial claim. If no complete proof is possible, give the strongest rigorous partial proof. You may freely use $...$ and \boxed{...} inside the proof — that is mathematics, not the output format.
</solution>
<self_evaluation>
Briefly audit the refined solution.
</self_evaluation>
<score>S</score>

where S is exactly one of 0, 0.5, or 1 (nothing else inside the score tag).
